options.text: sometimes a particle and other times a wave
options.html: sometimes a particle and other times a wave

questions.explanation: Light is an electromagnetic wave. It has a dual nature and behaves as both particle and wave.

questions.question_text: The propagation of light is best described by
questions.question_html: The propagation of light is best described by
questions.explanation: According to Maxwell's electromagnetic theory of light, light is an electromagnetic wave and it has a dual nature i.e it has both wave nature and particulate nature.

questions.question_text: Emission and absorption is best described by
questions.question_html: Emission and absorption is best described by
questions.explanation: Emission and absorption is best described by a particle model.

options.text: locus of all adjacent points at which the electric field is the same
options.html: locus of all adjacent points at which the electric field is the same

options.text: series of points on the wave with same amplitude
options.html: series of points on the wave with same amplitude

options.text: locus of all adjacent points at which the phase of oscillation is the same
options.html: locus of all adjacent points at which the phase of oscillation is the same

options.text: series of points on the wave with same frequency
options.html: series of points on the wave with same frequency

questions.correct_option_text: locus of all adjacent points at which the phase of oscillation is the same
questions.explanation: A locus of points, which oscillate in phase is called a wavefront. Thus a wavefront is defined as a surface of constant phase. The speed with which the wavefront moves outwards from the source is called the speed of the wave.

questions.question_text: A ray is an imaginary line

options.text: along the direction of travel of the wave
options.html: along the direction of travel of the wave

options.text: along the direction perpendicular to travel of the wave
options.html: along the direction perpendicular to travel of the wave

options.text: along the direction at an angle to the travel of the wave
options.html: along the direction at an angle to the travel of the wave

questions.correct_option_text: along the direction of travel of the wave
questions.explanation: The direction or path along which light energy travels in medium is called ray of light. Rays are the arrows perpendicular to the wavefront in direction of propagation of a wave.

options.text: Each point on a wavefront is a source of secondary waves
options.html: Each point on a wavefront is a source of secondary waves

options.text: No point on a wavefront is a source of secondary waves
options.html: No point on a wavefront is a source of secondary waves

options.text: Each point on a wavefront is travelling at any speed
options.html: Each point on a wavefront is travelling at any speed

options.text: Each point on a wavefront is a sink of secondary waves
options.html: Each point on a wavefront is a sink of secondary waves

questions.correct_option_text: Each point on a wavefront is a source of secondary waves
questions.explanation: According to Huygens principle, each point of the wavefront is the source of a secondary disturbance and the wavelets emanating from these points spread out in all directions with the speed of the wave. These wavelets emanating from the wavefront are usually referred to as secondary wavelets.

questions.question_text: According to Huygen's construction, relation between old and new wavefront is
questions.question_html: According to Huygen's construction, relation between old and new wavefront is

options.text: The new wavefront is the forward envelope of the secondary wavelet
options.html: The new wavefront is the forward envelope of the secondary wavelet

options.text: The new wavefront is inclined at an angle to the old wavefront
options.html: The new wavefront is inclined at an angle to the old wavefront

options.text: New wavefront is backward to old wavefront
options.html: New wavefront is backward to old wavefront

options.text: New wavefront is perpendicular to the old wavefront
options.html: New wavefront is perpendicular to the old wavefront

questions.correct_option_text: The new wavefront is the forward envelope of the secondary wavelet
questions.explanation: Huygens’ construction tells us that the new wavefront is the forward envelope of the secondary waves.

questions.question_text: Relation between ray and wavefront is

options.text: Rays are at acute angle to wavefront
options.html: Rays are at acute angle to wavefront

questions.explanation: Huygens’ construction tells us that the new wavefront is the forward envelope of the secondary waves. When the speed of light is independent of direction, the secondary waves are spherical. The rays are then perpendicular to the wavefronts.

questions.question_text: Approximate Doppler shift formula for light is
questions.question_html: Approximate Doppler shift formula for light is
questions.explanation: The fractional change in frequency Δ {tex}\nu{/tex} / {tex}\nu{/tex} is given by –v radial /c, where v radial is the component of the source velocity along the line joining the observer to the source relative to the observer. v radial is considered positive when the source moves away from the observer.

questions.question_text: When a beam of light is used to determine the position of an object, the maximum accuracy is achieved, if the light is
questions.question_html: When a beam of light is used to determine the position of an object, the maximum accuracy is achieved, if the light is
questions.explanation: The position of an object can be determined with maximum accuracy by using the light of shorter wavelength.

questions.question_text: Two sources of light are coherent if they have
questions.question_html: Two sources of light are coherent if they have

options.text: same frequency with a constant phase relationship
options.html: same frequency with a constant phase relationship

options.text: different frequency with a constant phase relationship
options.html: different frequency with a constant phase relationship

options.text: same frequency and changes phase randomly
options.html: same frequency and changes phase randomly

questions.correct_option_text: same frequency with a constant phase relationship
questions.explanation: Two sources are said to be coherent if they have exactly same frequency, and have zero or constant phase difference.

questions.question_text: Interference effects of light from two sources can be observed if the sources are
questions.question_html: Interference effects of light from two sources can be observed if the sources are

options.text: of different frequency and random phases
options.html: of different frequency and random phases

questions.explanation: If the two sources are coherent, then the phase difference φ at any point will not change with time, and we will have a stable interference pattern; i.e., the positions of maxima and minima will not change with time.

questions.question_text: If we have two coherent sources S 1 and S 2 vibrating in phase, then for an arbitrary point P constructive interference is observed whenever the path difference is
questions.question_html: If we have two coherent sources S1 and S2 vibrating in phase, then for an arbitrary point P constructive interference is observed whenever the path difference is
questions.explanation: If we have two coherent sources S 1 and S 2 vibrating in phase, then for an arbitrary point P whenever the path difference, S 1 P ~ S 2 P = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} (n = 0, 1, 2, 3,...), we will have constructive interference.

questions.question_text: If we have two coherent sources S 1 and S 2 vibrating in phase, then for an arbitrary point P destructive interference is observed whenever the path difference is
questions.question_html: If we have two coherent sources S1 and S2 vibrating in phase, then for an arbitrary point P destructive interference is observed whenever the path difference is
questions.explanation: If the point P is such that the path difference, S 2 P ~ S 1 P = {tex}(n + \frac{1}{2})\lambda {/tex} (where n = 0, 1, 2, 3, ...), we will have destructive interference and the resultant intensity will be zero.

questions.question_text: If we have two coherent sources S 1 and S 2 vibrating in phase with same amplitude, then for an arbitrary point P with path difference corresponding to {tex}\phi {/tex} , the resultant intensity if the intensity due to each is {tex}I_{0}{/tex} would be
